name: Kvasir
description: Kvasir was a being of immense wisdom in Norse mythology. He was not a god in the traditional sense, but rather a being born from the spittle of the Aesir and Vanir gods after they made peace following a long and arduous war. This unusual birth imbued him with unparalleled knowledge and understanding. Kvasir traveled throughout the world, sharing his wisdom and answering any question posed to him. His profound intellect made him a valuable asset to both gods and mortals. His story takes a tragic turn when he is murdered by the dwarves Fjalar and Galar, who then mix his blood with honey to create the Mead of Poetry. This magical mead grants anyone who drinks it the gift of skaldic verse and knowledge. The creation of the Mead of Poetry is central to Kvasir's legacy, as it becomes a source of inspiration and power for many figures in Norse mythology, including Odin himself, who eventually obtains the mead after a series of cunning exploits. Kvasir represents the power of knowledge and the importance of wisdom in Norse cosmology. His death highlights the potential for knowledge to be exploited and the lengths to which others will go to acquire it. He is a figure of both great potential and tragic fate, symbolizing the inherent risks associated with possessing extraordinary wisdom.
subtitle: Wisdom incarnate, murdered for his knowledge

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Kvasir
keyMyths.description: Kvasir was born from the spittle of the Aesir and Vanir gods as a symbol of their peace treaty. This unusual origin gave him unparalleled wisdom.

keyMyths.title: The Murder of Kvasir
keyMyths.description: The dwarves Fjalar and Galar murdered Kvasir and mixed his blood with honey to create the Mead of Poetry. This act was driven by greed for his knowledge.

keyMyths.title: The Mead of Poetry
keyMyths.description: The Mead of Poetry, created from Kvasir's blood, grants the drinker the gift of poetry and immense knowledge. Odin eventually steals the mead for the benefit of humankind.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including the story of Kvasir and the Mead of Poetry.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including the story of Kvasir and the Mead of Poetry.

sources.author: Lee M. Hollander
